title: Number of coral bleaching events by stage of the ENSO cycle
subtitle: [Coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching) typically occurs when water temperatures rise above the normal range for the coral's habitat.  This is more likely during El Niño stages of the [ENSO cycle](#dod:enso_cycle) when tropical sea temperatures are warmer.
caption:
Number of coral bleaching events by stage of the ENSO cycle
Coral bleaching typically occurs when water temperatures rise above the normal range for the coral's habitat. This is more likely during El Niño stages of the ENSO cycle when tropical sea temperatures are warmer.

The bar chart tracks the annual count of coral bleaching events from 1980 through 2016, categorizing each year by the prevailing phase of the El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) cycle. The vertical axis measures the number of events, ranging from zero to over 70, while the horizontal axis marks the timeline. Bars are color-coded to distinguish between El Niño years, shown in dark red; Moderate years, shown in purple; and La Niña years, shown in blue. The data shows a clear clustering of high-magnitude events during specific phases. The highest single value on the chart occurs in 1998, an El Niño year, where the count reaches approximately 73 events. Another significant peak appears in 2016, also an El Niño year, with roughly 54 events. In contrast, La Niña years generally display lower counts, though a notable exception exists in 2010, where the blue bar rises to approximately 53 events, nearly matching the 2016 total. Moderate years show variable activity, with a visible cluster of elevated counts between 2002 and 2006, peaking around 40 events in 2005. Earlier years in the timeline, such as 1980 and 1983, show lower totals compared to the later decades. The distribution of bar heights indicates that while bleaching events occur across all phases, the largest annual totals coincide with El Niño conditions, with the 1998 peak standing as the maximum value recorded in this dataset.
